name: Price Tracking
overview:
A price tracking system monitors product prices across multiple e-commerce websites, stores price history, and
alerts users when prices drop below their target. The system must crawl millions of product pages regularly,
detect price changes, store historical price data efficiently, and send timely alerts. Think CamelCamelCamel for
Amazon or Honey for general e-commerce.

inScope: Track prices for products across multiple retailers
Check prices every 1-6 hours per product
Alert users when price drops below target
Price check latency <30 minutes from actual change

items: Price check latency <30 minutes from actual price change to detection
Alert delivery within 5 minutes of price drop detection
99.9% availability for alert delivery; crawl delays are tolerable over missed alerts
Crawlers must respect robots.txt and per-domain rate limits to avoid being blocked
Price history stored efficiently: only write on change (~95% write reduction vs. every check)

entities.name: TrackedProduct
entities.schema:
{
  product_id: UUID,
  url: string,
  retailer: string,
  name: string,
  current_price: decimal,
  currency: string,
  last_checked: timestamp,
  check_frequency_hours: int
}

entities.name: PriceAlert
entities.schema:
{
  alert_id: UUID,
  user_id: string,
  product_id: UUID,
  target_price: decimal,
  notification_method: enum(EMAIL|PUSH|SMS),
  is_active: boolean
}

endpoints.response: { product_id, prices: [{ price, timestamp }], min_price, max_price }

approaches.title: Single Crawler, Fixed Schedule
approaches.description: One crawler checks all products every hour. 10M products × 1 check/hour = 2,778 checks/sec. A single crawler cannot handle this. Also, all products checked at the same time creates load spikes.
approaches.tradeoff: Single crawler cannot scale; load spikes from synchronized schedules.

approaches.title: Distributed Crawlers with Round-Robin
approaches.description: Multiple crawlers pull products from a queue in round-robin fashion. Better throughput but no prioritization. Popular products (many users tracking) should be checked more frequently than obscure products.
approaches.tradeoff: No prioritization; popular products checked at same frequency as obscure ones.

approaches.title: Priority Queue with Adaptive Frequency
approaches.description: Products are stored in a priority queue (Redis sorted set, score = next_check_time). Products with more trackers are checked more frequently (every 1 hour vs. every 6 hours). Crawlers pull the highest-priority products from the queue. After checking, the product is re-inserted with score = now + check_frequency. Crawlers respect robots.txt and rate limits per retailer domain.
approaches.tradeoff: Priority queue management adds complexity; adaptive frequency requires tracking user count

approaches.title: per product.
approaches.description: Price Change Detection Store Every Price Check Store the price from every check in the DB. At 2,778 checks/sec, this creates 240M rows/day. Most checks show no price change — wasteful storage.

approaches.title: Store Only on Price Change
approaches.description: Compare new price against last stored price. Only write to DB if price changed. Reduces writes by ~95% (prices rarely change). The last_checked timestamp is updated in Redis (not DB) on every check. Price history accurately reflects when prices changed, not when they were checked.
approaches.tradeoff: Requires reading last price before every write; Redis must store current price for fast

components.name: Alert API
components.responsibility: Accepts user alerts; creates TrackedProduct if new URL; stores alert in

components.name: Crawl Scheduler
components.responsibility: Manages priority queue (Redis sorted set); dispatches crawl jobs to workers

components.name: Crawler Workers
components.responsibility: Fetch product pages; extract prices (CSS selectors / ML extraction); publish to Kafka

components.name: Price Processor
components.responsibility: Consumes Kafka; compares with current price; writes to DB if changed; triggers alerts

components.name: Alert Service
components.responsibility: Checks if new price triggers any user alerts; sends notifications

components.name: Price DB (TimescaleDB)
components.responsibility: Time-series store for price history; hypertable partitioned by time

components.name: Redis
components.responsibility: Priority queue for crawl scheduling; current price cache for change detection

questions.title: 2. Anti-Bot Detection
questions.content:
Retailers use bot detection (Cloudflare, PerimeterX) to block crawlers. Mitigation strategies: (1) Rotate user
agents and IP addresses (residential proxy pool), (2) Respect crawl delays from robots.txt, (3) Use headless
Chrome for JavaScript-rendered pages, (4) Randomize crawl timing to avoid patterns. For retailers with strict
bot detection, use their official price APIs if available.

questions.title: 3. Price History Storage
questions.content:
Price history is stored in TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension for time-series data). The hypertable is
partitioned by time (monthly chunks). Queries for price history over a date range are efficient because only
relevant time chunks are scanned. Old data is compressed using TimescaleDB's native compression (10x
compression ratio for price data).

questions.title: 4. Alert Evaluation
questions.content:
When a price change is detected, the Price Processor queries the Alert DB for all active alerts for that product
where target_price >= new_price. For each matching alert, it publishes a notification event to Kafka. The Alert
Service consumes the event and sends the notification via the user's preferred channel. Alerts are deactivated
after triggering (one-time alerts) or kept active (recurring alerts).

questions.title: 5. Handling Retailer Rate Limits
questions.content:
Each retailer has a different rate limit tolerance. The Crawl Scheduler maintains a per-retailer rate limiter (token
bucket in Redis). Crawl jobs for a retailer are only dispatched when a token is available. If a crawler receives a
429 (Too Many Requests) response, it backs off exponentially and returns the product to the queue with a

delayed next_check_time.

keyFlows: ① User sets alert → Alert API → add product to Redis priority queue
② Crawl Scheduler dispatches jobs → Crawlers fetch product pages
③ Crawlers extract price → publish to Kafka
④ Price Processor: compare with Redis current price → write to TimescaleDB if changed
⑤ Price drop detected → check alerts → Notification Svc → email/push/SMS

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study TimescaleDB: how it extends PostgreSQL with hypertables, automatic time-based
partitioning, and native compression for time-series data.
Expert Note: Learn about web scraping at scale: how to handle JavaScript-rendered pages with
Playwright/Puppeteer, how to manage a residential proxy pool, and how to detect and handle bot detection
systems.
Expert Note: Understand Schema.org structured data: how JSON-LD and microdata embed
machine-readable product information in HTML, and how to extract it reliably.
Expert Note: Study token bucket rate limiting: how to implement a distributed token bucket using Redis,
and how to handle burst traffic while respecting average rate limits.
Expert Note: Learn about price elasticity and dynamic pricing: how retailers use algorithmic pricing to
respond to competitor prices, and how this affects the frequency of price changes.
